sacranento, CA 95B25

 AssiStance

Dear commissioner Liscom:
fhis is in response to your request for j-nformal assistance
.ron""..i.g your auties under the co;flict 
of interest provisions
of the poiilicaf 
Reforn Act (the rrasgrrl.l Since your Letter does
not concern a specific governmental decision, we treat your
,,""""rt as one ior infoinal assistance pursuant to Regulation
r}ize(c) (4) (D).2
QUESTI ON
Does your emptoyment for an insurance broker -';hose clientele
-inclucles persons or 6rganizations subject to the iurisdictior' 
o:
1..lrr: CaIj-ftrnia Horse nicing Board (the "racing board" ) preclude
;;; ;;;;-p;iiicipatins 
in iacins board decisions zrffecti-ns ti:ese
r: I ients?
CONCLUS IOI.I
I^Ihether or not you can participate in decisions affectin? t.-.e
insurance broker's ciients dlpends on each particular decision='s
tinancial effect on the broker.

FACTS
You are a nernber of the racingT board. You also are ernployed
as a Senior Vi-ce President for oland International fnsurance
Brokers (the t'brokerage firm").
The brokerage firm is based .in Los Angeles and has branch
offices j-n New York and Kansas City. 
It empl-oys approxinately 70
people and has annual- revenues of approximatety $10 mi1lion. 
The
-brok"rage firm's clientele consists solely of corporations and
organizitions. 
It has no individuals as clients. 
The firn's
operations are divided into two main sections: (1) Property and
cisualty Insurance (such as workers' compensation, public
liabilily, 
automobile and fire), 
and (2) Enployee Benefits. (such
as group medical, J,ife, travel accident, long tern disability 
and
pens j.on plans).
As Senior vice President of the brokerage firm, your day-to-
day activities 
are confined to servicing property and casualty
iniurances for eight corporate clients, none of rrhom are regulated
by the racing Uoaia. you are involved in neither the solicitation
oi n., busin6ss nor employee benefit coverages. You are paid a
salary, own 18 of the iirrn's stock, but do not participate in the
firm's profit sharing Prograns .
The brokerage firn has three clients who are subject to the
racing board's regulation. 
One is a racing association which pa;'s
approiimately $1900 annuall-y to the f irrn .f or a long terrn
dilability 
i-nsurance policy. 
Another is a racing association
which pay! approxinately $10,500 annually to.the.f irn for a group
medicai ina bental insuiance poticy. 
The thir:d is a uniorr of
horse racing employees which, through a trust, pays the f ir:m
$45,ooo annually for a group rnedical, life and dental insurance
liof icy. 
you haie no iniotvlment with any of these organizations
in your lrork for the brokerage firn.
You also indicate that, in the future, the brokerage firn
could provide insurance services for the conpany of .an individual
,n" rnay be regulated by the racing board in a capacity unrelated
to his compan! (he may, for instance, own a horse) ' In such a
case, you asX- us to a-ssume that the insurance policy will not be
="rri"id by you and is unreLated to the inclividual,s activities
that are regulated by the racing board.
ANALYSIS
Sectj.on 87100 prohibits each public official 
from rnaking'
participating in mai<ing, or attemPtl.S. to use his official
p"=iti"'" to influenc" gor".nrn.nta] decisior:s in which he has a
申


cur Fife No. I-9O-1"26
Page 3
financial interest. 
As a member of the racing board, you are a
public official 
and subject to this requirement.s
section 87103 states that an official 
has a financial
interest in a decision if it-is 
reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a rnaterial f i.nancial effect on the official 
or
on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirdct investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or nore.
(c) Any source of incone, other than gifts 
and
other than loans by a conmercial lending
institution 
in the regular course of business on
terrns available to the public rr'ithout regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or pronised to the public official
within L2 months prior to the tine when the
decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
ofriciil 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
ernployee, or hofds any position of management'
Because you own 18 of the stock in{, receive a salary fron
and are an employee of the brokerage firn, you have a financial
inierest in ti-re iirm under Section 87103. Therefore, you will 
be
pi..i"a"a 
from particj-pating in decisions of t-he racing board if
Lirey have a mat-erial financial effect on the brokerage firm'
Under the facts presented in your letter, 
any decisions ycu
make as a racing board mernber wilt have, at the most, an. indirect
effect on the biokerage firm, 
This is because the firm itself 
is
not reguLated in any way by the racing board' Rather, the ef f ec'-
on the firm, if any, wiff fe indirect in that the decision may
affect firm client! vrhich in turn may affect the insurance
premiums paid by the clients to the firn.
3 section 82048 defines public official, 
in pertinent part' as
Itevery member . . . of a stite or local government agency " ' "
sectiLn 82049 defines state agency as rrevery state office,
division, bureauf board and conmission, and the Legislature' "
(Emphasis added. )
4 wn p."t,rme that
exceeds 91,000 in
Lt of the brokerage firm's stock equals or
va Lue .
I


our File No. I-9 0-12 6
Page 4
tt is possi.ble that particular decisions by the racing board
wi.Il have ai indj,rect financial effect on the brokerage firm' 
For
example, a parti-cular racing board decision may foreseeably affect
. r.-"irrg aslociation in such a way that it loses :nembers and
thereby cancels its insurance coverage with the brokerage f irm'
fn sucir a case, where the indirect financial effects of the
Jecision on the firm are reasonably foreseeables,
Regulation L8702.2 wilI apply to determine whether the financial
effects are material.
Regulation ta702.2 (copy enclosed) sets forth the criteria
,r,r""r=oiy to determine whether a governmental decision has a
Iiaterial'financial 
effect on a business entity. 
This regulation
juages materiaf financial effect on the basis of the decision's
6rrect on a firm/s gross revenues, expenses or assets and
according to the maiket on which the firm's stocks are traded'
!'or exarnfl-e, if the brokerage firn's stock is traded on the New
york Sto;k Exchange, a racing board decision would have to affect
the firrn's annual gross revenues, assets or liabilities 
by
$25o,ooo or more 1or by $1 nillion or.nore if the firm is a
rortun" 5oo conpany), or affect the firm's expenses by $10o,0oo cr
more (or by $25O,oOO or more if the firn is a Fortune 500
comp"ny), in order for there to be a material financial effect by
the^delision on the firm' 
(Regulation 18702.2(a)') If .the firn's
stock is not traded on the 1iew-York Stock Exchange subdivisions
(b) through (S) of the regulation.wil-I apply, based on the
iinancial size of the brokerage firn'
You have not provided inforrnation indicating the narket or
which the brokerage firm's stock is traded' 
You also have not
presentecl us with a particular decision and its possible effec-; c:
Lh. brokerage firm's clients. 
Reference to Regulation 187o2'2
rii:.t assist you it.r determining the minimum do1lar anounts that
apply to the brokerage firm. 
Thereafter, you will have to analyze
eiltn'particular raciig board decision. and determine its
foresleable financial effect on the firrn's clients (and,
"iii.it"i', 
the firm) before deciding whether to participate in
that decision '

substantial ]ikelihood that the (
is no more than a mere possibility it iS not reasonab■
y
foreseeable.  (In re Thorner (■
